title: 2026 04 22 FEC
description: This is "2026 04 22 FEC" by New Zealand Parliament on Vimeo, the home for high quality videos and the people who love them.

chunks.text: Welcome back, Sarah and David. It's great to have you. We've got you for up to 30 minutes. We'll just maybe let you kick off with some introductory comments and things and we'll hand over for some Q&A. Okay, thank you. I'm Teina Kotakatua. I'm David Woods, the Chair of New Zealand Green Investment Finance and I'm joined by our Chief Executive, Sarah Minhinik. We were established to accelerate investment in New Zealand's decarbonisation and emissions reduction to help address a perceived market failure. One of our key aims was to secure co-investment of private capital to address the scale of the climate investment challenge. As you're aware, in April 2025, our shareholding ministers announced that the government had decided to wind down ends it gives existing portfolio of investments and ultimately exit

those investments over time. Ministers' expectations were and continue to be that we will meet all legal obligations and operate in a financially sustainable and self-funding manner during the wind down period. We're well advanced in that process and are achieving good results. We've returned total capital to the crown of over $500 million since our wind down plan was approved by our shareholding ministers both through cancelling uncalled shares and through cash distributions of $216 million. There is still work to do as we have some remaining investment positions to exit. These are complex structures that take time to manage. However, the board is confident in the team that we have and their ability to deliver the best outcome for the taxpayer in the circumstances. We will answer all questions from the committee as fully as possible

but we request members understanding that we must protect legally and commercially sensitive information. Thank you. Thank you very much for the work you've done. Obviously the committee is really interested to know more about your wind down process, what that looks like, particularly around existing investments, what's happening with those and also I think looking through the questions from you, there were 16 staff in terms of the transitional arrangements and what's happening with the staff just to start with. If I start with the staff, there were 16 I think when we were here last year, there are four today. The board is concerned to keep those staff

in place because of the complexity of the exit process from the remaining investments. Thank you. In terms of detail, I can't really talk about individual investments and how we've exited individual investments. We've sold about two-thirds of our total investments since the wind down plan was approved. We have a small number of both equity and debt investments. The total commitment still outstanding is about $80 million if I take undrawn commitments and investments. So of the investments that you have been able to sell, how many of them have been sold into New Zealand ownership? Sarah, do you want to? Almost everything. Okay, that's great. In terms of the hole that's going to be left in the market,

do you see that being built by other entities or do you think there will be a gap? Sorry if that's too difficult for you to answer. I think it's a policy question that we the government has to address. Fair enough. Someone else can go and look and beckon. Thank you, David, and to yourself for the work you've done in winding this down and to your team. I know it must be a testing time for them as they go through the process. Just in terms of timelines to get that hopefully $80 million or so, what's your expectations or the minister's expectations around when that would be liquidated and returned to government? We're in discussion with

Treasury and with the shareholding ministers. We expect the divestment stage of the process will finish during the next financial year. So the year to June 2027. We'd like it to finish sooner, but it's really a question of the process we have to go through. For instance, most of our equity positions have preemptive rights and that takes a time. So we're working through it as quickly as we can. And are you able to give us indication of I guess your investments? You'd have like a book value on those investments versus the realised value. Is there much difference in between those two? And if so, give us a sense of that. Let Sarah take the detail,

but the board is very satisfied that exiting as close to power as we can and the rest of them. Yeah, the exits have been pleasing. We were concerned this time last year when we were in the planning phases of the process that we could achieve elegant exits that would deliver value back to the Crown, but also be done in a way that was palatable for the portfolio companies. And I think we've helped that balance quite nicely. If we put solar zero to one side, because that's still ongoing, our blended recovery rate has been around 83 to 84 percent on average. Some of them have obviously been much higher. The recoveries on the debt have been very good. And I think that that is appropriate given that the portfolio was not in breach.

And for these assets, we are a required seller. So there will be a discount naturally. Yeah. Thank you very much. Todd Stephenson. Thanks for being here. Have you given any thought obviously over the life of the organisation and even in the windout? There's probably quite a lot of learnings and insights, et cetera. Have you given any thought to how that could be passed on, whether it's to Treasury or another central agency, so that what you've learned over the life of the organisation isn't kind of We're archiving all our records and we're happy to, at an appropriate time, have debriefing sessions with various people. But at the moment, I don't think that time is now. No, but you've obviously given us a thought, which I think is good. Because I think,

yeah, one of the things, again, quite often, the insights and experiences are lost across governments. I think just even giving that some thought is great. And we can follow up on that as well. I was actually going to ask a similar question. And you have a huge amount of knowledge about what is going to be a really important investment sector in New Zealand's future. So I'm really pleased to know that those insights are being captured. And I'm aware this might be the last time you come to the SLEE Committee. So thank you very much for your work. Just in terms of the wind down plan, just like what are the significant tasks that you say in that row ahead? And what do you see as some of the more significant tasks you still need to

complete? I think we're still in the mode of selling assets one by one. When we started, could we sell the whole portfolio? That wasn't an option that was available at a realistic price. So we have been divesting assets individually. We will probably not be able to sell all of them. So there will be a remaining set of assets. And we're in discussions with Treasury about what needs to happen then. Apart from the physical investments, then we have to wind down all our premises, our lease costs, silly things like subscriptions. But the whole thing has to be wound out and that will take a couple of months. Yeah. So I mean, obviously you

can't go into the details of specific assets. And I get that. But in terms of those discussions with Treasury about what will happen with the remaining assets, are you able to give us some options in a broad level that have been canvassed? I think it's really something that we need to ask to address. We are in detailed discussions with them. Yeah. Just going back to one of your earlier answers, Sarah, it was you, I think that said you'd kind of been sort of 84, 85% recovery on the value. It's fair to say that people know you have to sell, isn't it? So that there is a discount. In terms of the way we could have done this, was there, do you think there were all two different ways? Obviously, you bypassed the portfolio approach

because you saw there was going to be too much of a haircut taken by taking that approach. But if we've done this over a longer period of time, obviously holding on to the assets in different ways in different parts of government, do you think we could have actually preserved some of the equity or if the equity were done it differently? To be honest, I haven't really gone there in a great deal of thought because it was a policy decision and we were just tasked with executing it. These are decisions for the minister, so I appreciate that. But thank you for your work. Thanks, Megan. We're going to go to Dr Dave Lawson. Yeah, just a learning question really, giving your reflection on it about scope and complementarity with other funds,

such as the Provincial Growth Fund and of course then it morphed into the Regional Infrastructure Fund. Sorry, David, we're back here. Can you talk into the microphone? Yes. Thank you. Did you hear all of that? Yes. So yeah, that's the question, the complementarity and scope question because there's some learnings there, I'm sure, where we're crossing over each other or there certainly are learnings. We have had detailed discussions with Treasury about different areas and complementarity, but again, I'm afraid it's a policy question. Yeah, I understand that. Really, it's from our perspective, it's a learning question. What did you learn from that environment? I think on a more general level, we're very friendly with all of our

opposite numbers at all of these places. So David and I regularly talk to our counterparts at ECAR and NZGCP and Kanawa and our RK, various other places, and there's quite a good spirit, I would say, amongst the execs and the board members. So I suppose to the extent that's helpful, that has been an ongoing theme of people engaging with each other. Right. It's really following on from your question earlier, Megan, framing it slightly differently. Maybe a question for me, possibly for you as the Chair, David, with regard to some of the entities which you're potentially going to struggle to completely wind down, do you anticipate a transfer of that value into another government entity via Treasury's guidance or something like that?

I think that that is clearly an option, but it's a, we will continue selling divesting assets until we're told to stop and we'll then deal with whatever's left over. I'm just mindful that you've got an end date on June 30th or June 26th, that's that runways running short. Is that? No, I think. But you speak to everything to be materially completed by the 30th of June? I think that's what we thought a year ago, but our new SPE has it running until the end of this year, potentially early into the calendar year next year, but we would like to have it finished by the end of this year. So we may in fact see you again next year.
I don't know. Well, thank you very much for that. We do appear to be at the end of our questions. So thank you for the ongoing work and the updates. Do they really appreciate it?
